Jamshed11's blog

Codeforces Round 1081 (Div. 2) Editorial
By Jamshed11, 5 weeks ago,

2192A — String Rotation Game

Hint 1

By performing the operation, try to break a block into 22 blocks.

Solution

The only way to increase the count of blocks is to break a block into 22 blocks by performing an operation such that some characters of a block are present at the start of the string and some characters at the end of the string. For us to be able to achieve this, there must exist at least one block with a size greater than or equal to 22. But, if in the original string, the first and the last character are already equal, this means a block is already broken into 22 block (in some other rotation, it will be one block). Hence in this case the answer will be the score of the original string.

Inshort, if first and the last character of the string are equal, or no 22 adjacent characters are equal, then the answer will be score of the original string. Else the answer will be score of the original string +1+1.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code: 363907571

2192B — Flipping Binary String

Hint 1

What is the condition for -1?

Hint 2

If the length of the string is odd and the count of 1 is odd, then and only then the answer will be -1.

Solution

First of all, flipping a bit an even number of times is equivalent to not flipping the bit at all, and flipping a bit an odd number of times is equivalent to flipping the bit just one time. So, to make all the bits equal to 00, we have to make sure we flip all the 00 bits an even number of times and all the 11 bits an odd number of times.

One of the ways to achieve this is, if the count of 00 bits in the string is odd, then apply the operation on all the 00 bits. If the count of 00 bits in the string is even and the count of 11 bits in the string is also even, then apply the operation on all the 11 bits. If the count of 00 bits in the string is even and the count of 11 bits in the string is odd, then after applying any operation, the count of 00 bits will always stay even. But the length of the string in this case is odd which means we cannot make all the bits equal to 00. Hence in this case the answer will be −1−1.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code: 363909084

2192C — All-in-one Gun

Hint 1

If an entire magazine is used, then the operation does not change the total damage dealt or the time used by that magazine. Hence, the operation mainly impacts only the last magazine.

Hint 2

For us to be able to kill the enemy using only ii bullets from the last magazine, what is the necessary condition? Also, what would be the most optimal operation for this?

Solution

First, calculate the total damage an entire magazine deals, which is sum of the array aa. We will have to use at least h/sumh/sum magazines. Now, the remaining health of the enemy would be hh modulo sumsum. If the remaining health is 00, then we don't need to worry about the operation.

If the remaining health is greater than 00, then we will try to perform an operation that can minimise the number of bullets required in the last magazine. For every ii from 11 to nn, we will check if we can kill the enemy using only ii bullets from the last magazine. When checking for the index ii, the most optimal operation will always be to swap min(a0,a1,…ai)min(a0,a1,…ai) and max(ai+1,ai+2,…an)max(ai+1,ai+2,…an). If min(a0,a1,…ai)>max(ai+1,ai+2,…an)min(a0,a1,…ai)>max(ai+1,ai+2,…an), then it is optimal to not perform the operation for this ii. After performing the operation (or not performing), if sum(a0,a1,…ai)sum(a0,a1,…ai) becomes less than or equal to the remaining health, then it is possible to kill the enemy using only ii bullets from the last magazine. The smallest ii satisfying this condition gives us the most optimal answer.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code: 363909342

2192D — Cost of Tree

Hint 1

How to solve the problem for all subtrees without considering the operation?

Hint 2

Answer of hint 11: If cost(i)cost(i) represents the cost of subtree with root ii, and sum(i)sum(i) represents the sum of value of all nodes present in the subtree with root ii, then cost(i)cost(i) is the summation of cost(j)+sum(j)cost(j)+sum(j) for all nodes jj that are children of node ii.

Hint 3

Now for the operation, for a chosen node uu, once the edge between node uu and the parent of node uu is removed, always choose node vv as the node present in the subtree with root as parent of node uu, and having maximum d(d(parent of node uu, v)v) (distance from partent of node uu). Do this for all possible node uu.

Solution

This problem can be solved in a single DFS on the tree. In the DFS, each node will calculate 22 things for it's subtree, cost without performing operation and the maximum cost with operation already performed in the subtree.

For calculating cost without performing operation, as compared to the child, the value of d(r,i)d(r,i) (distance of ii from rr) increases by 11 for the parent, where ii are all the nodes present in the subtree of child. d(r,i)d(r,i) increasing by 11 means adding aiai once, as per the equation of the cost of the tree. And adding aiai once for all nodes present in the subtree means adding sum of values of all nodes present in the subtree once. So, cost of parent == summation of cost of jj + sum(j)sum(j) for all nodes jj that are children of the parent, where sum(j)sum(j) represents the sum of value of all nodes present in the subtree of jj.

For calculating the maximum cost with operation already performed in the subtree, what the operation does is, it changes the value of d(r,i)d(r,i) for all nodes ii present in the subtree of node uu (nodes uu and vv are described in the operation). To maximize the cost, we have to increase d(r,i)d(r,i) by the maximum possible value. And if the value of d(r,i)d(r,i) is increased by xx, for all nodes ii present in the subtree of node uu, it means the cost of tree increases by sum(u)∗xsum(u)∗x. Hence, for a chosen node uu, node vv should be the node with the maximum depth in the remaining tree. At this point, if node vv does not lie in the subtree of parent of node uu, then it will always be optimal to choose parent of uu as node uu (because this increases sum(u)sum(u)). We can keep doing this, until node vv lies in the subtree of parent of node uu. Hence, if we try performing operation for each node uu, and node vv is always chosen as the node with the maximum depth present in the remaining subtree of parent of node uu, and we take the maximum cost across all these operations, we will always get the maximum cost of the tree we can achieve. In the DFS, for subtree of rr, we only need to calculate cost by performing operations for node uu, where node uu are children of node rr. For all other nodes present in subtree that can be the node uu, the maximum cost with operation already performed in the subtree of children of node rr would have already taken care of this.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code: 363909632

2192E — Swap to Rearrange

Hint 1

Graph problem?

Hint 2

Eulerian cycle?

Solution

Make aa a rearrangement of bb means the frequency of all numbers present in array aa should be equal to the frequency of all numbers present in array bb. For this to be possible, the combined frequency of all numbers present in array aa and array bb should be even. If at least one number has combined frequency as odd, the answer will be −1−1.

Now, if the answer is not −1−1, let's see how to perform the operations. Create a graph with nn nodes and nn directed edges from node aiai to bibi. The operation swap aiai and bibi means the direction of the ii-th edge is reversed in the graph. This means, for each edge any direction is possible. Hence, we can consider the graph as an undirected graph, which we need to convert to a directed graph. In other words, we need to decide the direction of all the edges.

In the directed graph, the indegrees and outdegrees of a node ii actually mean the frequency of ii in array aa and array bb respectively. Our problem is to make the frequency of each number in array aa equal to array bb, which means to make the indegrees of each node equal to its outdegree.

Since, the combined frequency of all numbers is even, it means the degree of each node in the undirected graph is even. This means the graph contains Eulerian cycles. Each connected component can be treated like a separate graph (because it is possible that our graph is not a single connected component), and find Eulerian cycle in all these graphs. Keep assigning direction to the edges in the direction used in the Eulerian cycle and hence accordingly perform the operations.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code: 363911188

2192F — Fish Fight

Hint 1

DP?

Hint 2

Try solving the problem in 22 phases. Phase 11, when Alice's fish and Bob's fish are not adjacent to each other, and phase 22, when they are adjacent to each other.

Solution

First, let's solve the problem for phase 11 (fish AA (Alice's fish) and fish BB (Bob's fish) are not adjacent to each other):

We will maintain 22 separate dpdp, one for each player. Both the dpdp work independently of each other. Each dpdp will have 22 states ll and rr, denoting the fish has eaten all other fishes present in the range [l,r][l,r], and dp[l][r]dp[l][r] stores the probability of this happening (without considering the turns played by the other player's fish). This means, the size of the fish has increased by the sum of bibi for all ii present in the range [l,r][l,r] and ii not equal to the original position of the fish (xx for fish AA and yy for fish BB).

From dp[l][r]dp[l][r] we can go to dp[l−1][r]dp[l−1][r] and dp[l][r+1]dp[l][r+1]. But if both the adjacent fishes (fish (l−1)(l−1) and fish (r+1)(r+1)) have a larger size and can't be eaten, then in this case the player's fish gets eaten and loses. Hence this directly contributes to the answer without going to the phase 22 (only if due to this the fish AA wins). But in this case, we can't directly add dp[l][r]dp[l][r] to the answer as we need to ensure that the other fish is still alive.

Let's say, dpAdpA and dpBdpB represents dpdp of fish AA and fish BB respectively, and lala and lblb represent ll of fish AA and BB respectively, and rara and rbrb represent rr of fish AA and BB respectively.

If from dpB[lb][rb]dpB[lb][rb] the fish BB can't eat any of the adjacent fish and hence fish BB is going to get eaten, then

probabiliityB=dpB[lb][rb]probabiliityB=dpB[lb][rb]

At this point, fish BB occupies subarray of length rb−lb+1rb−lb+1, which means fish BB has played a total of rb−lbrb−lb turns. Corresponding to this, the fish AA would have played rb−lb+1rb−lb+1 turns (since they play alternate turns starting from fish AA). So, we need to find the probability that the fish AA is not a neighbor of fish BB and fish AA is still alive (fish AA occupies subarray of length rb−lb+2rb−lb+2). Considering fish AA is present to the right of fish BB, for fish AA to not be a neighbour of fish BB, lala should be at least rb+2rb+2. And for fish AA to occupy subarray of length rb−lb+2rb−lb+2, rara should be equal to la+(rb−lb+1)la+(rb−lb+1). Hence,

probabiliityA=probabiliityA= summation of dpA[la][ra]dpA[la][ra] (for all la≥rb+2la≥rb+2 and corresponding ra≤nra≤n)

We can similarly calculate when fish AA is towards the left of fish BB. We can maintain prefix sum and suffix sum to get these summations in O(1)O(1) time.

We will add probabiliityA∗probabiliityBprobabiliityA∗probabiliityB directly to the final answer without going to the second phase.

No need to do these things when fish AA can't eat any of the adjacent fish and hence fish AA is going to get eaten, because this increases the answer by 00 as we need to calculate the probability of fish AA winning and not the probability of AA losing.

Now, let's solve the problem for phase 22 (fish AA and fish BB are adjacent to each other):

For this, we will maintain a single dpdp. The states of the dpdp will be lala and the total number of turns played (combined of both fish AA and BB). Since both the fishes are adjacent to each other, from only these 22 states we can calculate all 44 states (la,ra,lb,rbla,ra,lb,rb). In phase 11, when fishs AA and BB get adjacent to each other, the corresponding probability (probabilityA∗probabilityB)(probabilityA∗probabilityB) can be added to this dpdp of phase 22. If the total number of turns played is cc, then in this dpdp, either we can move to dpdp with state c+1c+1 (and correspondingly the other state lala can also change), or the fish eats the other player's fish, or it gets eaten by it's adjacent fishes. In the latter 22 cases, if the fish AA wins, then we can add the probability of this happening in the final answer.

Time complexity: O(n2)O(n2)

Space complexity: O(n2)O(n2)

I solved problem B with the same idea as the solution, probably some minor mistakes. Can anyone help point it out? Thanks.

(Python btw)

Spoiler
t = int(input())

for j in range(t):

    n = int(input())

    s = input()

    ones = [i+1 for i, ch in enumerate(s) if ch == "1"]
    zeros = [i+1 for i, ch in enumerate(s) if ch == "0"]

    if len(ones) % 2 == 1:
        print(-1)

    elif len(ones) == 0:
        print(0)

    elif len(zeros) % 2 == 1:
        print(len(zeros))
        print(*zeros)

    else:
        print(len(ones))
        print(*ones)

    void solve()
{
    ll n, h, k;
    cin >> n >> h >> k;

    vll a(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        cin >> a[i];
    }

    ll sum = accumulate(all(a), 0ll);

    ll multiple = h / sum;
    ll res = (k + n) * multiple;
    // cout << "multiple : " << multiple << endl;
    vll suffix_max(n + 1, 0);
    // suffix_max[n] = 0;
    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--)
    {
        /* code */
        suffix_max[i] = max(suffix_max[i + 1], a[i]);
    }

    ll cursum = multiple * sum;
    if (cursum >= h)
    {
        cout << res - k << endl;
        return;
    }

    ll range_min = 1e10;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        res += 1;
        range_min = min(range_min, a[i]);
        cursum += a[i];
        if (cursum + max(0ll, suffix_max[i + 1] - range_min) >= h)
        {
            cout << res << endl;
            return;
        }
    }
}

For E, one doesn't even need to find Euler’s cycle. We can try to pair up all the numbers and turn every problem into another easier problem which every number shows up twice in the combined array of a and b. This can be done by simply sequentially assigning a new number to each pair.

For 2192C - All-in-one Gun,

My solution: Array a will be sorted in non increasing order in the start, let's say, till the index i, the largest i elements of array a are present in non increasing order. After that the order breaks (at index i + 1).

Let the largest, farthest (from index i + 1) element present at index j. So, my claim is that, if we try to swap all indices (from i + 1 -> j — 1) with the index j (always one of the swapped indices is j), then, we can get the answer.

What's the issue with my approach, getting wrong answer?

why my code is getting wa for c problem pls can anyone tell ?

~~~~~~ //this is code

void solve()

{

int n, health, k;

cin >> n >> health >> k;

vector<int> a(n);

 multiset<pair<int,int>> ms;

int sum = 0;

for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)

{

    cin >> a[i];

    ms.insert({a[i],i});

    sum += a[i];

}

vector<int> pref(n+1,0);

for(int i=0;i<n;i++){

    pref[i+1]=pref[i]+a[i];

}

int ans=0,add=n;

// for(auto it : ms){

//     cout << it.ff << " " << it.ss << endl;

// }

// cout << endl;

// for(auto it : pref){

//     cout << it << " ";

// }

// cout << endl;

int step=health/sum;

if(health%sum==0){

    ans=step*n+(step-1)*k;

    cout << ans;

    return;

}

else{

    int rem=health%sum;

     ans=(step)*n + (step)*k;

    auto it = lower_bound(pref.begin(),pref.end(),rem);

    int dist=it-pref.begin();

    add=min(add,dist);

    for(int i=0;i<n-1;i++){

        ms.erase({a[i],i});

        // cout << a[i] << "  " << i << endl;

        auto v=*ms.rbegin();

        // cout << (v.ff) << " " << (v.ss) << endl;

        int diff=v.ff-a[i];

        if(diff<=0)continue;

        int l=0,r=n;

        int found=-1;

        while(l<=r){

            int mid=l+(r-l)/2;

            int find=pref[mid];

            // cout << find << endl;

            if(mid<=v.ss && mid>=(i+1)){

                find+=diff;

            }

            if(find>=rem){

                found=mid;
                r=mid-1;
            }
            else{
                l=mid+1;
            }
        }
        // cout << "found" << " " << found << endl;
        if(found!=-1){
            add=min(add,found);
        }
        // cout << add << endl;
    }
}
// dbg(add);
// dbg(ans);
cout << ans+add;

}

~~~~~

In ques. 2192D - Cost of Tree,

My solution: Consider a node nd (we want to calc. answer for nd). If u and v are in different child subtrees of nd, I claim that u must be immediate child of nd, and v must be the farthest node from nd.

If u and v are in same subtree (let's say in subtree of immediate child x), x would have calc. answer for itself, nd can use his answer for its answer calc.

    0

    I didn't read your code but this observation is not always correct. if node u has one child, then you can't do an operation on its child. My initial observation was similar to yours but i noticed the problem while implementing.

    Fix

    you can maximize between the best operation for u (zero of no operations exist) and the best operations in the nodes of u's subtree. you can simply do that by storing the effect of the operation on every node and at every node maximize between its operation and the best operation for its children. and by a node's operation i mean the best operation that can be done while this node is the root of the subtree.

0

Problem D bonus:

What if the operation doesn't refrain from choosing a node uu outside of the subtree of node rr?

What node should we consider?

Answer

We would need to consider the node uu outside the subtree of the node rr with the greatest cost. Then, we would need to link that node to node vv, which we choose to be the deepest node inside the subtree of node rr. Additionally, uu can't be on the path r→1r→1.

How can we do this?

Hint

How can we flatten the tree into an array and perform queries quickly on that array?

Answer

Use Euler Tour to flatten the tree and use Segment Tree to perform queries

Answer

We use a Segment Tree Max and an Euler Tour to flatten the tree into an array of 2n2n elements. If you don't know what an Euler Tour is, read this.

Then, we would perform DFS from the root, and on our path, we update the values corresponding to that node on the Segment Tree to be −∞−∞ (or just add −∞−∞ to them) so that we could avoid taking those nodes. We also want to avoid taking nodes that are in the subtree of node rr so we would add −infty−infty to the range [in[r],out[r]][in[r],out[r]] on the Segment Tree.

And we take the max value on the array, link that to the deepest node of the subtree of node rr, just like the original editorial, compare that to the case when we take node uu inside of the subtree of node rr.

Overall complexity O(nlogn)O(nlogn).

→ Reply

    Lets say node 1 is parent of node 2, and currently you are thinking about choosing node 2 as u. "choose parent of u as node u" means instead of choosing node 2 as u, choose node 1 as u.

can someone help me check my C :v Bruh i really dont see any issue T_T wrong in test 2 :V in test case 3252

edit: i just relize my wrong :V fixed 

// this is code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; 
typedef long long ll;
int main() {
	ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(nullptr);
	cout.tie(nullptr); 
    int t; 
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        int n,h,k; 
        cin >> n >> h >> k; 
        ll a[n+5];
        ll q[n+5];
        ll b[n+5]; 
        cin >> a[1];
        q[1] = a[1]; 
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
            cin >> a[i]; 
            q[i] = q[i-1] + a[i];
        }
        b[n] = a[n];
        for (int i = n-1; i >= 1; i--) b[i] = max(b[i+1], a[i]); 
        ll t = h/q[n]; 
        ll r = h - t*q[n]; 
        int u = 0;
        if(b[1] >= r) u = 1;
        else {
            for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
                if (q[i-1] + b[i] >= r) {
                    u = i; 
                    break; 
                }
            }
        }
        long long ans; 
        if (t == 0) ans = u; 
        else {
            ans = t*n + (t-1)*k; 
            if (r > 0) ans+=(k+u); 
        }
        cout << ans << '\n'; 
    }
    return 0; 
}

                dp[u] = max(dp[u], c[u] + (maxDepth - depth)*sum[v]);
            }
            dp[u] = max(c[u] + (dp[v] - c[v]), dp[u]); // !!!
        }
    }

hello i have a different way of solving b that thought was interesting:

let the number of 1's & 0's in the original string be x and y, in each turn/move you can go from (x,y) to (y-1,x+1) and (y+1,x-1) if x,y>0, and you want then value of either x or y to become zero(by decreasing it 1 by 1)

so you can set it on the number of 1's but there is a catch since everytime your doing the move you are flipping the spots of x & y at the end the one that you choose to decrease by one each turn might end up on the number of 0's ,

how you can calculate this is if x mod 2 = 0 the end value of x can end up on the number of 1's or if y mod 2 = 1 then the end value of y can end up on the number of 1's if both are true you choose the minimum and if none are true the output is -1

i know i could've explained better but as a beginner i did the best i could if a better programmer sees this and would like to refine it i would love to answer any questions they have

anyways here is the code i wrote during the contest:

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;


int main() {
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--) {
        int n;
        string s;
        vector<int> ap;
        vector<int> bp;
        cin >> n;
        cin >> s;
        
        for (int i=0;i<n;i++) {
            if (s[i]=='1') {ap.push_back(i);}
            else {bp.push_back(i);}
        }
        int a=ap.size();
        int b=bp.size();
        if (b%2!=1 && a%2!=0) {
            cout << -1 ;
        }
        else if (b%2==1 && a%2!=0) {
            cout << b << endl;
            for (int i=0;i<b;i++) {
                cout << bp[i]+1 << ' ';
            }
        }
        else if (b%2!=1 && a%2==0) {
            cout << a << endl;
            for (int i=0;i<a;i++) {
                cout << ap[i]+1 << ' ';
            }
        }
        else if (b%2==1 && a%2==0) {
            int x=min(a,b);
            cout << x << endl;
            if (x==a) {
                for (int i=0;i<a;i++) {
                    cout << ap[i]+1 << ' ';
                }
            }
            else {
                for (int i=0;i<b;i++) {
                    cout << bp[i]+1 << ' ';
                }
            }
        }
        cout << endl;
    }
}

Here are my notes and core understanding of Problem E: The inherent even-degree property of undirected Euler circuits is what guarantees the element balance between the two arrays. In the ideal case, the circuit forms a closed ring where every node has a degree of exactly 2, with edges running either a[i]→b[i] or the reverse. In practice, when building the graph, some nodes may have edges in the opposite direction from the ideal, resulting in a non-ideal state. But as long as we track these nodes, we can construct a valid ideal Euler circuit by simply choosing a consistent ideal direction for the edges. Elements can flow freely within the circuit, and thanks to the in-out conservation brought by the even-degree property, they will always end up in a balanced state. My implementation: 363982694

in div2 C I couldn't understand the solution so made a prefix sum array while replacing minimum element till with maximum element available after that point. It is accepted but can it be simplified?

// this is code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define ll long long
int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        ll n , h , k;
        cin>>n>>h>>k;

        vector<ll> a(n);
        vector<ll> pre(n);
        vector<ll> most(n);

        ll sum = 0;
        ll mx = 0;
        ll idx = 0;
        for(ll i = 0 ; i < n ; i++){
            cin>>a[i];
            sum+=a[i];
            if(a[i] > mx){
                mx = a[i];
                idx= i;
            }
        }
        ll temp = a[n-1];
        for(int i = n-1 ; i >= 0 ; i--){
            most[i] = temp;
            temp = max(temp , a[i]);
        }

        ll presum = 0;
        for(int i = 0 ; i < n ; i++){
            temp = min(temp , a[i]);
            presum += a[i];
            if(temp > most[i] || i == n-1){
                pre[i] = presum;
                continue;
            }
            pre[i] = presum - temp + most[i];
        }

        ll time = (h/sum)*n;
        if(h/sum > 0){
            time += ((h/sum))*k;
        }
        
        if(h%sum == 0){
            cout<<time-k<<'\n';
            continue;
        }

        h = h%sum;
        if(h <= mx){
            cout<<time+1<<'\n';
            continue;
        }

        for(int i = 0 ; i < n ; i++){
            ll o = h-pre[i];
            ++time;
            if(o <= 0){
                cout<<time<<'\n';
                break;
            }
        }
        
    }

    return 0;
}

Thank You

→ Reply
